subtopics.title: Articles
common_misconceptions: All nouns ending in -e are feminine
Articles are not important in French
Partitive articles are the same as indefinite
subtopics.teaching_note: Pronunciation guide: le [lə], la [la], les [le]. Masculine nouns generally take le/un, feminine take la/une. Articles contractes: de + le = du, de + les = des, a + le = au, a + les = aux. Partitive: used for uncountable nouns (du pain, de l'eau). Agreement is crucial in French - article, adjective, and noun must agree in gender and number.

subtopics.title: Verbes au Present
common_misconceptions: French verb conjugation is random (there are patterns)
Avoir and etre are only used as main verbs
subtopics.teaching_note: Premier groupe (-er verbs): parler → je parle, tu parles, il/elle/on parle, nous parlons, vous parlez, ils/elles parlent. Deuxieme groupe (-ir verbs with -issons): finir → je finis, nous finissons. Troisieme groupe (irregular): etre (je suis, tu es, il est, nous sommes, vous etes, ils sont), avoir (j'ai, tu as, il a, nous avons, vous avez, ils ont), aller (je vais, tu vas, il va, nous allons, vous allez, ils vont). être and avoir are auxiliary verbs used in compound tenses.

subtopics.title: Vocabulaire Quotidien
common_misconceptions: Months are capitalized in French (NO, only proper nouns)
subtopics.teaching_note: Pronunciation guide: Salutations: Bonjour [bɔ̃ʒuʁ] (good day), Bonsoir [bɔ̃swaʁ] (good evening), Salut [saly] (hi/bye - informal). Numbers: 70 = soixante-dix (60+10), 80 = quatre-vingts (4×20), 90 = quatre-vingt-dix. Days: lundi, mardi, mercredi, jeudi, vendredi, samedi, dimanche (lowercase). Family: pere, mere, frere, soeur, fils, fille. Nigerian context: French is Nigeria's second official language due to neighboring Francophone countries (Benin, Niger, Cameroon). Useful for ECOWAS trade and diplomacy.

subtopics.title: Reading Comprehension
common_misconceptions: Answer in English when question is in French
subtopics.teaching_note: Technique: 1) Read passage once for gist. 2) Read questions. 3) Scan for specific information. 4) Answer in complete French sentences. Common question words: Qui? (who), Qu'est-ce que? (what), Où? (where), Quand? (when), Pourquoi? (why), Comment? (how). Pay attention to cognates (words similar to English): information, nation, culture, impossible.
